steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The claim is that Newland follows social conventions dictated by other men. The quotation must show those men exercising power over Newland's behavior — not Newland observing them, not his internal thoughts, and not his superiority over them.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — ‘"Though there was already talk...of a new Opera House which should compete in costliness and splendour with those of the great European capitals, the world of fashion was still content to reassemble every winter in the shabby red and gold boxes of the sociable old Academy [of Music]."’: Describes a setting detail about the Opera House; says nothing about other men dictating Newland's behavior. ✗
  • Choice B — ‘"It surprised [Newland] that life should be going on in the old way when his own reactions to it had so completely changed."’: Expresses Newland's private surprise; no other men are directing him here. ✗
  • Choice C — ‘"Newland Archer felt himself distinctly the superior of these chosen specimens of old New York gentility; he had probably read more, thought more, and even seen a good deal more of the world, than any other man of the number."’: Reverses the relationship — Newland feels superior to the men, not controlled by them. ✗
  • Choice D — ‘"Singly [the men around Newland] betrayed their inferiority; but grouped together they represented 'New York,' and the habit of masculine solidarity made him accept their doctrine on all the issues called moral."’: The men's collective force ('masculine solidarity') compels Newland to accept their rules — exactly the power dynamic the claim describes. ✓
That confirms D is correct.
